name: Alto do Ladiairo and Hermisende Loop
name_original: Lubián – vuelta por Hermisende y Castromil – Zona Especial de Conservación Peña Maseira
description: This difficult 27.4-mile road cycling route through the Peña Maseira Special Area of Conservation offers challenging climbs and scenic views
region_intro: Road cycling around Lubián offers routes through rugged landscapes and varied terrain in the Zamora province of Spain. The region is characterized by significant elevation changes, with routes traversing protected areas like the Peña Maseira Special Area of Conservation. Cyclists can expect to encounter dramatic terrain, serene glacial lakes, and charming villages along the way. The area provides a challenging environment for road cyclists seeking routes with considerable climbs. Best road cycling routes around Lubián The most popular road cycling route is Porto and San Sebastián Dam Loop from Lubián, a 60.6 miles (97.5 km) trail that takes 5 hours 1 minute to complete. This difficult route features over 5,500 feet (1,690 meters) of elevation gain through rugged landscapes and past glacial lakes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Alto do Ladiairo and Hermisende Loop, a difficult 27.4 miles (44.1 km) path. This route offers scenic views as it climbs over 3,300 feet (1,019 meters) through the Peña Maseira Special Area of Conservation. Local road cyclists also love the Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria – Puebla de Sanabria Castle loop from Requejo, a 27.6 miles (44.5 km) trail leading through charming villages and historical sites, often completed in about 2 hours 17 minutes. Road cycling around Lubián is defined by rugged landscapes, significant elevation changes, and routes passing through protected areas and charming villages. The network offers options for moderate to difficult ability levels, with a focus on challenging climbs. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Lubián's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling routes around Lubián?
A: Road cycling around Lubián is characterized by significant elevation changes and rugged landscapes, making most routes challenging. Out of the 9 routes available, 7 are classified as difficult, and 2 are moderate, requiring a good level of fitness.

Q: What kind of terrain and elevation gains can I expect on Lubián's road cycling routes?
A: You can expect rugged landscapes, often traversing protected areas like the Peña Maseira Special Area of Conservation. Routes feature considerable climbs; for example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1024476822/porto-and-san-sebastian-dam-loop-from-lubian" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Porto and San Sebastián Dam Loop from Lubián&lt;/a&gt; includes over 1,690 meters of elevation gain, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40008253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lubián Village – Portela da Canda Pass loop from Lubián&lt;/a&gt; has over 2,290 meters of ascent.

Q: What natural features and landmarks can I see while road cycling near Lubián?
A: The routes often pass through stunning natural scenery. You might encounter serene glacial lakes, dramatic terrain, and protected areas. Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4708534" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sotillo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4945816" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Scenic landscape in Parque Natural de Montesinho&lt;/a&gt;, and various reservoirs like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/872404" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Serra Serrada Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any waterfalls accessible from the road cycling routes?
A: Yes, the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4708534" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sotillo Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, located in Sotillo de Sanabria, is a natural highlight within the Sanabria Lake Natural Park. It's particularly spectacular in spring and after rain, and can be a rewarding stop near some cycling routes.

Q: Do any road cycling routes pass through protected natural areas?
A: Yes, several routes traverse protected areas. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1024600884/alto-do-ladiairo-and-hermisende-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto do Ladiairo and Hermisende Loop&lt;/a&gt; climbs through the Peña Maseira Special Area of Conservation, offering scenic views within this protected landscape.

Q: Are there charming villages or cultural sites along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes pass through quaint villages, offering cultural glimpses and opportunities for breaks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38158174" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria – Puebla de Sanabria Castle loop from Requejo&lt;/a&gt;, for example, leads through charming villages and historical sites, including the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3834076" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parish of Saint James the Apostle&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available around Lubián?
A: Yes, most of the road cycling routes around Lubián are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1024476822/porto-and-san-sebastian-dam-loop-from-lubian" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Porto and San Sebastián Dam Loop from Lubián&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20148319" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto de Covelo – Viewpoint of Viana do Bolo loop from A Mezquita-A Vilavella&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Lubián?
A: The region's rugged landscapes and natural parks, like Montesinho, offer a dreamlike and quiet environment. While specific seasonal recommendations aren't provided, spring and autumn generally offer milder temperatures and beautiful scenery, making them ideal for challenging rides. The Sotillo Waterfall is particularly impressive in spring and after rain.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Lubián?
A: Road cyclists using komoot often praise the challenging climbs and the stunning natural beauty of the region. The routes are appreciated for traversing rugged landscapes, passing serene glacial lakes, and offering scenic views through protected areas like Peña Maseira.

Q: Are there any viewpoints along the road cycling routes in Lubián?
A: Yes, the challenging routes often lead to rewarding vistas. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1024600884/alto-do-ladiairo-and-hermisende-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto do Ladiairo and Hermisende Loop&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, offers scenic views as it climbs through the Peña Maseira Special Area of Conservation. You can also find specific viewpoints like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7989709" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Puente Porto reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Lubián?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes available around Lubián, catering to various levels of difficulty, with a focus on challenging climbs and scenic landscapes.
tips: Brief but pronounced 2 km cycling climb with an average gradient of 8.9%. The path begins to ascend as soon as you cross the 18th century masonry bridge over the Tuela River on the outskirts of Hermisende.

The Portela da Canda Pass, also known as the Canda Pass or simply A Canda, is a mountain pass of great geographical, historical, and cultural value that connects Galicia (province of Ourense) with Castile and León (province of Zamora).

Located in the Sierra Segundera mountain range, within the Galician Massif, it has traditionally been a crossing point for travelers, merchants, and pilgrims.
It forms part of the Sanabrés Way of St. James, an extension of the Silver Route (Vía de la Plata), and is the entry point into Galicia for pilgrims coming from the south.
The surrounding area features Jacobean sculptures and wayside crosses (cruceiros) that guide the route, the work of the artist Nicanor Carballo.
Small mountain village at 1000m altitude on the trade route between Castilla y León and Galicia with traditional rural houses made of stone and wood.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route begins and ends in Lubián Village. You should be able to find parking within the village itself, which serves as a convenient starting point for this circular tour.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for road cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a 'Hard road ride' due to its mountainous character. You can expect significant climbs and descents with considerable elevation changes, typical of the Sanabria region. While roads are generally well-paved, they are winding and will test cyclists' endurance. Be prepared for challenging gradients.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll be immersed in the natural beauty of the Sanabria mountains, featuring open fields, forests, and dramatic landscapes. The route offers views of traditional stone villages like Lubián Village, Hermisende, and Castromil, nestled amidst ancient chestnut forests. Hermisende is particularly picturesque, situated in a small valley alongside the Tuela River.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or villages will I pass through on this route?
faqs.answer: The route takes you through several charming villages and past notable features. You'll cycle through Lubián Village, Hermisende (with its Hermisende Bridge and San Ciprián Climb), and Castromil. You'll also traverse the Portela da Canda Pass, offering expansive views.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically from late spring through early autumn. During winter, access roads to Lubián and Hermisende can be affected by snow and ice, making conditions challenging and potentially unsafe for road cycling. Always check local weather forecasts before you go.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' with a substantial elevation gain of over 1,000 meters. It requires a very good level of fitness and is best suited for experienced road cyclists seeking a challenging ride, not for families or beginners.

faqs.question: Is the Special Area of Conservation Peña Maseira dog-friendly for cyclists?
faqs.answer: While the route passes through the Special Area of Conservation Peña Maseira, specific regulations for dogs within the SAC for cyclists are not widely publicized. Generally, dogs are permitted in natural areas but should always be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and respect other users. Please ensure your dog is accustomed to cycling alongside you and that you clean up after them.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle through the Peña Maseira Special Area of Conservation?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route through the Special Area of Conservation Peña Maseira. However, as it is a protected natural area, please adhere to all environmental regulations, stay on marked roads, and practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several villages, including Lubián, Hermisende, and Castromil. These villages may offer small cafes or restaurants where you can refuel. However, it's always advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on a challenging route like this, as services might be limited or have irregular opening hours.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official cycling or walking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the [CIMA ZM02] Ladiairo - Marabón (38%) and [CIMA ZM06] Parque Eólico Padornelo * Lubián (14%). It also briefly crosses parts of the Camino Sanabrés (East) and Mtb Camino Sanabrés (Este) / Mtb Camiño Xabrés (Leste).

faqs.question: What should I be aware of regarding road conditions or traffic?
faqs.answer: The roads are generally quiet asphalt roads with minimal traffic, allowing for an immersive cycling experience. However, given the mountainous terrain, expect winding roads and steep gradients. During winter, be aware that snow and ice can affect road conditions, particularly around Lubián and Hermisende.

waypoints.name: Hermisende Bridge and San Ciprián Climb
